quoteFirst: There was a Christmas tree for him and for the eight McGillicuddies, and the day was so full that Mrs. Fortescue found it hard to get time in which to give Kettle the necessary wigging for taking the baby from his bed and carrying him out of doors
quoteLast: in the evening because he waked up and said "Horsey."  In vain Kettle pleaded "fo' Gord--" always a forerunner of a tarradiddle--that he "didn't have no notion on the blessed yearth as Miss Betty would mind," and also wept copiously when Mrs.
title: Betty at Fort Blizzard
author: Seawell, Molly Elliot, 1860-1916; Frederick, Edmund, 1870-1949 [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: "If I go," said Dino, rising from his chair, "I shall take the express train to Scotland
quoteLast: to-night, and I shall see Miss Murray to-morrow morning."
title: Under False Pretences: A Novel
author: Sergeant, Adeline, 1851-1904

quoteLast: , and immediately began to make preparations to advance. At 11 A.M. the troops moved forward on the plank and old turnpike roads. The enemy was soon encountered on both roads, and heavy skirmishing with infantry and artillery ensued, our troops pressing steadily forward.
title: The Rise and Fall of the Confederate Government, Volume 2
author: Davis, Jefferson, 1808-1889

quoteLast: in the evening we crossed Platte river and took quarters for the night in the little town of Smithville. Here Colonel Smith had bivouacked on the night of the 15th, the rebels he was pursuing having done so the night before.
title: Recollections with the Third Iowa Regiment
author: Thompson, Seymour D. (Seymour Dwight), 1842-1904

quoteFirst: Late hours are life-shortening things, but I would rather run all risks, and sit every night--at some places I could name--wishing in vain at eleven o'clock for the entrance of the supper tray, than be always up and alive
quoteLast: breakfast as I am here. We have a scheme to reconcile these things. We have an offer of a very low-rented lodging a mile nearer town than this.
title: Mary Lamb
author: Gilchrist, Anne (Anne Burrows), 1828-1885

quoteFirst: ' First and second class prisoners are employed in this prison--which is no Castle of Indolence--at drill, shot exercise, the crank, cleaning the passages and other parts of the premises from six o'clock A.M. to six o'clock P.M.; and those of the third class from six o'clock A.M. to
quoteLast: P.M.; with the exception of regular times for parades, chapel, and meals.
title: Chambers's Journal of Popular Literature, Science, and Art, No. 729, December 15, 1877
author: Various; Chambers, Robert, 1802-1871 [Editor]; Chambers, William, 1800-1883 [Editor]

quoteLast: a fine, cold rain, which the wind drove in slanting lines, began to fall, and the furniture on the cart had to be covered over with tarpaulins. Some steaming cups of coffee stood on the kitchen-table, and Jeanne sat down and slowly drank hers up; then rising:
title:
The works of Guy de Maupassant, Vol. 5
Une Vie and Other Stories
author: Maupassant, Guy de, 1850-1893

quoteFirst: Therefore it is best I come to a halt in this play of words, although it would be possible to fill page after page with what we thought, and said, and did during that long, painful night’s journey; for, although we had set out, as nearly as we could judge, at about
quoteLast: in the evening, the sun was two hours high in the heavens before we were come to Presque Isle, so nearly exhausted that Alec fell upon the shore, unable to move hand or foot, when we were arrived in front of my home.
title: With Perry on Lake Erie
author: Otis, James, 1848-1912; Stecher, William F. (William Frederick), 1864-1940 [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: What weather! You seldom see the like! I wrote to you that we had passed the meridian of Petersburg. I was mistaken. We only passed it to-day
title:
From Libau to Tsushima
A narrative of the voyage of Admiral Rojdestvensky's fleet to eastern seas, including a detailed account of the Dogger Bank incident
author: Politovskii, Evgenii Sigizmundovich, 1874-1905; Godfrey, Frederick Rowlandson, 1867- [Translator]

quoteFirst: The clerks in the mercantile houses are kept pretty closely to business, from
quoteLast: till near the same hour at night, holidays excepted, which is fagging work.
